article_title: I spent 10 years in Evernote until this free note-taking app pulled me away
core_thesis: After a decade of using Evernote, the author switched to Notion because Evernote's free plan became too restrictive (limited to one notebook and 50 notes) and aggressively pushed upgrades, while Notion offered unlimited notes, templates, better customization, and multi-device access for free, making it a superior alternative for personal journaling and note-taking.
detailed_summary: The author, a long-time Evernote user since 2016, decided to switch after encountering several limitations on the free plan: only one notebook allowed, a cap of 50 notes, and constant ads urging an upgrade. These restrictions hindered his growing need to organize notes by topic and write daily journal entries. After testing alternatives, he chose Notion because its free tier removed note limits, provided a template marketplace for different use cases (e.g., fitness, emotions), offered aesthetic customization with icons and cover images, and allowed access from multiple devices. Transferring notes was straightforward via Notion's desktop site. The author concludes that Notion restored the joy of journaling without the frustrations of Evernote's free plan.

entities.name: Nate Pangaro
entities.role: Author and tech enthusiast who switched from Evernote to Notion

entities.name: Evernote
entities.role: Note-taking app the author used for 10 years but left due to free plan limitations

entities.name: Notion
entities.role: Free note-taking app the author switched to, offering unlimited notes, templates, and multi-device access

key_data_points: Evernote's free plan restricts users to one notebook and a maximum of 50 notes, which becomes impractical for users with diverse topics or daily journaling habits (🟢 relevant to your productivity: if you use note-taking apps for study or project organization, these limits can hinder your workflow).

Evernote's free plan displays frequent ads prompting upgrades, disrupting the user's focus and writing flow (🟢 relevant to your student needs: constant interruptions can reduce productivity during study sessions).

Notion's free plan imposes no limit on the number of notes, allowing the author to write daily journal entries instead of weekly summaries (🟢 relevant to your productivity: unlimited notes support extensive note-taking for courses, research, or personal projects).

Notion offers a built-in template marketplace with free and paid templates for various use cases like fitness and emotional journaling, including guided prompts to overcome writer's block (🟢 relevant to your student needs: templates can help structure study notes, project plans, or reflection journals).

Notion allows customization with icons and cover images, making notes more visually engaging and easier to identify at a glance (🟢 relevant to your productivity: visual organization can improve recall and navigation).

Notion's free plan supports access from multiple devices (mobile and desktop), enabling seamless switching between writing on a phone and continuing on a laptop (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops: multi-device sync is essential for managing documentation or configurations across environments).
methodology_steps: Identify the limitations of your current note-taking app (e.g., note caps, notebook restrictions, intrusive ads).
Research alternative apps that offer free plans with fewer restrictions; consider features like unlimited notes, templates, and multi-device sync.
Download and test several apps (e.g., Notion) to evaluate their usability and feature set against your needs.
Transfer existing notes to the new app; for Notion, use the desktop site (not mobile) to import notes from Evernote.
Organize notes using Notion's templates and customization options (icons, cover images) to improve structure and visual appeal.
Utilize multi-device access to write on the go and continue on a desktop, ensuring seamless workflow.
conclusions: Evernote's free plan has become too restrictive for daily use, with a 50-note cap and single notebook limit making it impractical for users with expanding note-taking needs. The constant upgrade ads further degrade the user experience.
Notion provides a superior free alternative by removing note limits, offering a template marketplace, and allowing multi-device access, which together enable more flexible and enjoyable note-taking.

The switch from Evernote to Notion improved the author's journaling habits, allowing daily entries and better organization through templates and customization, demonstrating that free tools can meet advanced note-taking requirements.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your productivity and student needs, as it compares two popular note-taking apps and highlights how free-tier limitations can hinder organization and writing habits. As a student, you can apply Notion's unlimited notes and templates to structure course notes, research, or project plans. The multi-device sync is also valuable for accessing study materials across your phone and laptop. If you currently use Evernote or another restricted app, this article provides a clear rationale and practical steps for switching to a more flexible free tool.
extra_advice: Consider exploring Notion's template marketplace for academic templates (e.g., lecture notes, assignment trackers) to further streamline your study workflow. You can also integrate Notion with other tools like calendars or to-do lists to create a centralized productivity hub.
